description: Registry of reusable configuration concepts shared across analysis and derivation methods. A library Configuration declares conforms_to = <concept_id> to reference a concept defined here; the expander (Python/JS) fills in dataType, defaultValue, description, enumValues, and codings from the registry at load time. For enum-typed concepts that carry per-value scope metadata (e.g., multiplicity_adjustment), the library Configuration also declares applicable_scopes, and the expander filters the catalog down to values whose scope matches. This design keeps the canonical definition in one place and prevents library drift.

alpha.description: Significance level (Type I error rate) for hypothesis tests. Method-invariant: applies to any hypothesis test.
alpha.notes: Always equals (1 - conf_level) by convention, but the two are kept as separate configurations because tests and interval estimates may be parameterised independently.

conf_level.description: Confidence level for interval estimates. Method-invariant: applies to any interval estimate.
conf_level.notes: Always equals (1 - alpha) by convention. Kept separate from alpha for parameterisation flexibility.

multiplicity_adjustment.description: Catalog of multiplicity adjustment procedures. Each procedure declares its own scope of statistical validity. A library Configuration referencing this concept also declares applicable_scopes (a list of scope ids from this concept's scopes dictionary); the expander filters the catalog down to values whose scope is in that list. This keeps methodologically incorrect combinations impossible by construction — e.g., a Cox model with applicable_scopes = ['universal'] cannot surface Tukey HSD in its enumValues, because Tukey's scope is 'anova_pairwise_means' and the resolver never includes it. Multiplicity adjustment is a per-output-class post-processor (R's emmeans::pairs(adjust=...), SAS's LSMEANS ... adjust=...); valid_placement is ['output_class'] only, enforced by the expander to prevent method-level misuse.

universal.description: Applies to any collection of p-values with Uniform(0,1) nulls. Operates on the sorted p-value vector via combinatorial or order-statistic rules; does NOT reference the test statistics' distributions. Its error-rate guarantee (FWER or FDR) is invariant to the underlying test.
valid_for: Cox proportional hazards (hazard ratio Wald tests)
any correctly-computed p-value across endpoints or families

anova_pairwise_means.description: Pairwise comparisons of group means in ANOVA-style linear models. Critical values derive from the joint distribution of pairwise contrasts assuming normal errors, homogeneous variances, and independent observations.
valid_for: MMRM LSMeans all-pairwise contrasts (within a fixed effect)
invalid_for: Cox hazard ratio pairwise contrasts (wrong reference distribution)

anova_many_to_one.description: Many-to-one comparisons (all treatments vs a single control) in ANOVA-style linear models. Critical values derive from a multivariate-t with an equicorrelated structure (correlation 1/2 in the balanced case) arising from the shared control group's sampling variation.
invalid_for: any many-to-one comparison not coming from a linear model with homogeneous variances

linear_contrasts.description: Arbitrary linear contrasts in linear models with homoscedastic normal errors. Critical values from the F-distribution.

None.description: No multiplicity adjustment. p-values are reported as computed by the base test.

Bonferroni.description: Family-wise error rate (FWER) control via the Bonferroni inequality. Multiply each p-value by the number of tests. Simplest upper bound; makes no dependence assumptions; conservative.

Holm.description: FWER control via Holm's step-down procedure. Uniformly more powerful than Bonferroni; makes no dependence assumptions.

Hochberg.description: FWER control via Hochberg's step-up procedure. Assumes independence or positive regression dependence of p-values.

Hommel.description: FWER control via Hommel's closed-testing procedure. More powerful than Hochberg under the same dependence assumptions; more computationally intensive.

Sidak.description: FWER control via Sidak's correction. Slightly more powerful than Bonferroni but assumes independence of p-values.

BH.description: False Discovery Rate (FDR) control via Benjamini-Hochberg step-up procedure. Assumes independence or positive regression dependence of p-values.

BY.description: FDR control via Benjamini-Yekutieli. Valid under arbitrary dependence structure; more conservative than BH.

Tukey.description: Tukey Honestly Significant Difference. Critical values from the studentized range distribution, derived under ANOVA assumptions: normal errors, homogeneous variances, independent observations, all-pairwise comparison of group means in a balanced (or Tukey-Kramer unbalanced) linear model. Not valid outside that setting — applying it to Cox hazard ratios, logistic log-odds, or GLM contrasts from non-linear models gives critical values that don't correspond to the true contrast distribution, so the claimed FWER does not hold.

Dunnett.description: Dunnett's test for many-to-one comparisons (all treatments vs a single control). Critical values from a multivariate t-distribution with an equicorrelated structure (correlation 1/2 in the balanced case) arising from shared control-group sampling variation. The correlation structure is a property of ANOVA-style mean comparisons in a linear model; it does not hold for Cox hazard ratios, logistic log-odds, or GLM contrasts from non-linear models.

Scheffe.description: Scheffé's method for arbitrary linear contrasts in linear models. Critical values come from the F-distribution and assume homoscedasticity and normality of residuals within a single linear model. Not applicable outside that setting.

NewmanKeuls.description: Newman-Keuls sequential range test for ANOVA all-pairwise comparisons. Known FWER inflation issues beyond three groups; ANOVA-specific.

REGWQ.description: Ryan-Einot-Gabriel-Welsch Q: range-based ANOVA all-pairwise procedure. Same structural assumptions as Tukey.

conformance_rules.description: Rules the load-time expander applies when resolving a library Configuration that declares conforms_to. These rules make the registry the single source of truth; library method files cannot drift.
rules: If Configuration.conforms_to == <concept_id>, the expander fills in missing fields on the Configuration from concepts[<concept_id>]: name (defaults to concept_id), dataType (from concept.dataType), description (from concept.description), defaultValue (from concept.typical_default).
Local Configuration fields always override inherited ones: a library method may set its own defaultValue or description without losing the conforms_to link.

For enum concepts with a 'values' catalog, the Configuration MUST also declare applicable_scopes (array of scope ids from concept.scopes). Missing applicable_scopes defaults to ['universal'] if that scope exists, else errors.
The expander computes enumValues = [value_name for value_name, value_def in concept.values.items() if value_def.scope in applicable_scopes], preserving the order defined in concept.values.
The expander computes codings = union of concept.values[v].codings for every v in the filtered enumValues.
If Configuration.conforms_to references a concept_id not in this registry, the expander raises an error at load time.
If Configuration.applicable_scopes references a scope_id not in the concept's scopes dictionary, the expander raises an error at load time.
The expander runs BEFORE schema validation. The schema sees fully-populated Configurations; library files can be minimal.
